You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? — React Basics: Building a TodoList (Part 1) - (10)
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now?

In this chapter we're going to actually build something small. The whole point is to pull together everything we've covered so far, because knowledge with zero output is honestly kind of useless. So let's roll up our sleeves and get some practice in. We'll build a TodoList that adds items, marks them done with a checkbox, shows a count, and clears everything at once.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Basics - useContext - (9)

In the earlier chapters we got a first look at how React passes data between components with Props. The thing is, Props always flow from the top down. That one-way flow keeps your data predictable, but it also creates a classic headache: you end up passing data way too deep. So in this chapter we'll meet something new that fixes exactly that — useContext.
React Basics: Getting to Know useEffect (and Side Effects) — (8)

Time to meet a new React Hook: useEffect. The name looks a little odd at first — "use effect"? Effect of what? In this post we'll first get a handle on what a Side Effect actually is, then walk through every flavor of useEffect: running after every render, running once, watching specific values, and the cleanup function. I'll also compare each one to its Vue equivalent so it clicks faster.
React Basics: useState, the One Hook You Absolutely Have to Get (7)

State is one of the most important things in React Hooks. How important? In short, the moment you need to change something that shows up on screen, you can't escape React state. In this post I'll walk through useState — starting from Vue's v-model so the Vue folks have something familiar to anchor to — then dig into how React handles data, why it's called one-way binding, and what types you can throw at useState.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Components & Props - (6)

Time to talk about components. If you're a Vue developer, the component concept is probably second nature to you already. So what about React? How do React components work? In this post we'll walk through React components, nested components, Props (and the fact that they're read-only), and how to fake Vue's slot behavior using children.
React Basics: Event Handling, and Why React Feels Like Plain JavaScript (5)

Let's talk about event handling in React, because honestly you're going to be doing it constantly once you start building anything real. I'll start with a quick Vue refresher (since a lot of us come from Vue), then show how React does clicks, changes, submits, key presses, focus and blur. The short version: there's no .prevent modifier here — you call preventDefault yourself, which is exactly why people say writing React feels like writing plain JavaScript.
You've Got to Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - JSX Basics - (4)
You've Got to Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now?

You've probably heard people throw around the term "JSX" — but what actually is it? It kind of sounds like one of those things you absolutely have to learn if you want to write React. In this post we'll get to know what JSX really is, walk through its basic syntax, and cover keys, expressions, array spreading, comments, and how styles and classes work differently from plain HTML.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? — React Basics: Class? Hooks? — (3)

Function Component? Class Component? React Hooks? What even are these things? In this post we'll get to know the two ways of writing React and what makes them different. We'll convert the same counter from a Class Component into a Function Component, and then walk through the two rules you have to follow when using React Hooks.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? — React Basics: Hello React (2)

We've already taken a quick look at how React differs from Vue in terms of syntax. So now it's time to actually start getting to know React. In this post we'll set up a React environment with just a CDN, render our first "Hello React", and peek at how JSX and React.createElement relate under the hood.
You'll Have to Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Basics - What Even Is React? - (1)
You'll Have to Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now?

No matter what, you've got to start by getting comfortable with React's basic concepts and a bit of its background. That groundwork makes it way easier to spot the differences when you pick up other frameworks later. I'm a Vue developer myself, so I'm writing this with the mindset of someone adding one more framework to the toolbox — which means you'll see a few side-by-side comparisons between Vue and React syntax. Mostly I want to make this an easy on-ramp for fellow Vue devs. Enough preamble, let's get into it.
